full_response:
Let S be the “laboratory’’ inertial frame in which the centre of the merry-go-round is at rest.  
Suppose S measures the merry-go-round to make

 f ≡ N rotations per second  (angular period T = 1/f).

Choose one marked point P on the rim (radius R).  
Define two events

 A : P lies on the +x–axis at t = 0,  
 B : the next time that P again lies on the +x–axis, t = T.

In S the coordinates are  
 A : (t, x, y, z) = (0, R, 0, 0)  
 B : (t, x, y, z) = (T, R, 0, 0)

so Δx = Δy = Δz = 0 and Δt = T.

Now look at an arbitrary inertial frame S′ that is related to S by a standard Lorentz boost of speed v = βc along the x–direction.  
(The result for a boost in any other direction is analogous.)

Lorentz transformation:

 t′ = γ ( t − βx/c ),  x′ = γ ( x − βct ),  γ = 1/√(1−β²).

Apply it to the two events.

Event A : t′_A = γ (0 − βR/c) = −γβR/c  
Event B : t′_B = γ (T − βR/c) = γT − γβR/c

Hence the time interval between the same two physical events in S′ is

 Δt′ = t′_B − t′_A = γT .             (1)

Therefore the period measured in S′ is longer by the Lorentz factor γ and the frequency is correspondingly smaller:

 T′ = γT  ⇒  f′ = 1/T′ = f/γ .        (2)

Exactly the same reasoning holds for a boost in any direction: because the two events A and B occur at the same spatial point in S (Δ⃗x = 0), the Lorentz transformation always gives Δt′ = γΔt.

Interpretation  
Rotations-per-second is just another clock frequency.  Any periodic process carried by an object that is moving with speed v with respect to an inertial observer appears slowed by the usual time-dilation factor γ.  Two inertial observers that are in relative motion therefore do not agree on the numerical value of “rotations per second’’ of the merry-go-round.

(There is no single boost that can make the whole disk non-rotating; one can only change its translational state.  Hence no inertial frame can make the rotation frequency vanish; they can only disagree about its value.)

\boxed{\text{No.  Different inertial observers see different rotation rates; an observer who moves with speed }v\text{ relative to the merry-go-round measures }f' = f/\gamma,\; \gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-v^{2}/c^{2}}.}

Confidence: 77%
